disease.name: Pediatric Asthma (Acute Exacerbation)

ageGate.hardRule: Asthma is unlikely under 12 months; bronchiolitis dominates. Probability increases after 24 months. If <12 months and wheezing, default to bronchiolitis.

disease.note: Pediatric asthma exacerbation management. Use PRAM (Preschool Respiratory Assessment Measure) for children aged 2-6 years and PASS/clinical assessment for older children.

historyFeatures.note: ≥2 wheeze episodes in last 12 months, especially if triggered by URIs
Reported or observed improvement after salbutamol is a strong positive predictor
URI triggers are the most common exacerbation trigger in preschool children

examFeatures.note: Polyphonic expiratory wheeze is hallmark. Absent wheeze (silent chest) is ominous.
No wheeze audible – ominous sign of severe obstruction and impending respiratory failure.
>10 mmHg in older children indicates severe obstruction

immediateDangerSigns.description: >200 bpm (2-3 yrs) or >180 bpm (4-5 yrs) or >150 bpm (>5 yrs)

riskFactors.description: Personal history of eczema, food allergy, or allergic rhinitis.
Previous PICU admission or intubation for asthma.

riskFactors.note: Most common trigger in preschool children

bedside.name: Pulse oximetry
bedside.indication: all acute wheeze — SpO2 is key severity indicator

bedside.name: Peak Expiratory Flow Rate (PEFR)
bedside.priority: if age >6 years and able to cooperate
bedside.note: Helps assess severity (% predicted) and response to treatment. PEFR <50% predicted = severe, <33% = life-threatening.

laboratory.name: Blood gas (venous or arterial)
laboratory.priority: if severe or not responding to initial therapy
laboratory.indication: assess hypercapnia, acidosis. Normal/high PaCO2 in a distressed child is concerning.
laboratory.note: Normal PaCO2 in severe asthma may indicate fatigue — rising PaCO2 is a sign of deterioration

laboratory.name: FBC
laboratory.indication: if fever or toxic appearance to evaluate infection

laboratory.name: CRP
laboratory.indication: if fever present to rule out bacterial infection

imaging.name: Chest X-ray
imaging.indication: first severe episode, atypical presentation, poor response to bronchodilators, suspected foreign body, suspected pneumothorax or pneumomediastinum, fever
imaging.findings: Hyperinflation, peribronchial cuffing. Rule out pneumonia, foreign body, pneumothorax.

management.protocolReference: Kenya Paediatric Guidelines + GINA 2025 pediatric acute exacerbation principles + PRAM-based stepwise therapy

criteria.sign: wheeze only, no indrawing, no tachypnoea

treatment: Salbutamol 2-4 puffs via MDI + spacer every 4-6 hours PRN
Prednisolone 2 mg/kg PO once daily for 3 days (max 20 mg/day for 2-5 yrs, 30 mg/day for 6-12 yrs)
Alternative: Dexamethasone 0.15-0.3 mg/kg PO single dose (max 12 mg) as effective as 3-day prednisolone
Review inhaler technique and spacer use
Advise trigger avoidance
Return if: symptoms worsen, requiring >4-hourly reliever, night waking

criteria.sign: wheeze + chest indrawing or tachypnoea for age

treatment: Nebulised salbutamol 2.5 mg (or 6 puffs via MDI + spacer) every 20 minutes for up to 3 doses in first hour
Ipratropium bromide 250 mcg nebulised (or 2 puffs 80 mcg via MDI) WITH salbutamol for first 3 doses in first hour if poor initial response
Prednisolone 2 mg/kg PO once daily for 3-5 days (or Dexamethasone 0.15-0.3 mg/kg single dose PO)
Oxygen to keep SpO2 ≥94% if hypoxic
Monitor SpO2, RR, heart rate, wheeze intensity, PRAM/PASS score after each bronchodilator
moderateSeverity.reassessment: If lack of response after 3 doses: increasing RR, worsening SpO2, PRAM not improving → escalate to severe protocol.

criteria.sign: Any danger sign: SpO2 <90%, cyanosis, unable to drink/talk, silent chest, exhaustion, AVPU abnormal

treatment: Admit urgently to HDU/ICU
Oxygen to maintain SpO2 ≥94%
Continuous nebulised salbutamol (2.5-5 mg/hour) or back-to-back MDI (6-12 puffs) via spacer every 15-20 min
Ipratropium bromide 250-500 mcg nebulised with first 3 salbutamol doses
Prednisolone 2 mg/kg PO/IV daily (max 60 mg/day) for 5 days
IV Magnesium sulfate 40 mg/kg (max 2 g) infused over 20 minutes if poor response to initial therapy
IV fluids: maintenance only, avoid overload (SIADH risk)
Monitor for fatigue, rising PaCO2, silent chest — prepare for non-invasive or mechanical ventilation
If no improvement, consider differentials: pneumonia, foreign body, cardiac failure, TB, anaphylaxis

ifNoResponseToMg: Consider IV salbutamol 15 mcg/kg bolus then 0.1-1 mcg/kg/min infusion in ICU setting
Consider IV aminophylline 5 mg/kg loading then 0.5-0.9 mg/kg/hour infusion (only in ICU with monitoring)
Early intubation and mechanical ventilation if deteriorating: rising PaCO2, exhaustion, decreasing consciousness

mdiVsNebulizer.mdiSpacer: Preferred method. 1 puff = 100 mcg. Use 2-4 puffs for mild, 6-12 puffs for moderate-severe. More efficient than nebulizer in cooperative children.
mdiVsNebulizer.nebulizer: Indicated if child too distressed for MDI + spacer, or severe exacerbation requiring continuous therapy. 2.5 mg salbutamol (0.5 mL of 5 mg/mL + 2.5 mL NS) per dose.

dischargeCriteria: SpO2 ≥94% on room air for 4-6 hours

positiveResponse.criteria: Decreased wheeze, reduced RR and indrawing, improved SpO2, improved PEFR/PRAM/PASS score.
positiveResponse.action: Asthma probability confirmed. Step down bronchodilator frequency. Discharge with action plan.

noResponseAfter1_2Hours.criteria: Persistent severe distress, worsening hypoxia, no improvement in wheeze or air entry, PRAM not decreasing.
noResponseAfter1_2Hours.action: Re-evaluate diagnosis: increase probability of pneumonia, foreign body, anaphylaxis, cardiac failure, vocal cord dysfunction.

recurrenceWithin48Hours.criteria: Return of wheeze/distress after initial improvement and discharge.
recurrenceWithin48Hours.action: Consider inadequate maintenance therapy, persistent trigger, or alternative diagnosis (e.g., foreign body). Start or optimize ICS if not already on.

mimics.note: Consider in infant with wheeze and failure to thrive.

complications.management: ICU, IV bronchodilators, possible mechanical ventilation

disease.emergencyPriority: If any life-threatening feature (silent chest, SpO2 <90%, exhaustion, cyanosis), immediate ICU-level care
